Lesson one, things to look for from the seller. The goal here in doing due diligence is to simplify the game. There's a tremendous amount of data to look at. Some data is more critical than others. Too many real estate investors think all information is exactly the same and it's not. This was my comment about the bed bugs. Bed bugs don't really matter any more than termites would. You have to handle it, you got to get rid of it, of course, but it's not a reason not to buy. Too many real estate investors think of the due diligence in the opposite way they should be. They see it as overwhelming and that's something you must avoid. Due diligence isn't overwhelming. Due diligence is the easy part of your deal. Taking on bad deals, that is the hard part. If not, the impossible part. So do your due diligence. The first step is to ask yourself these critical questions. What kind of real estate do you want to be in? Is it B, C? Is it A? A means a lower return. C means a higher return with more risk, more problems, and more tenant issues. When you go to the double A, you're going to have a very demanding tenant, but you'll probably have a lower crime. You understand what I'm saying? Do you want to be in the game of real estate or not? Real estate that's paying income and paying down the debt over long periods of time come with risk and it comes with responsibility. And somebody going to have to handle those bed bugs, the termites, the tenants, and those toilets. There will be plumbing issues. Don't be surprised. My mom first told me when I was getting invested in real estate, oh my god, toilets are going to overflow. People are going to call you at midnight. I'm like, I hope they call me. She's like, what? I hope they call me. I hope people are actually in the apartment paying me. See what part of ridiculous do you want? What part of a problem do you want? Number two, do you want that deal? Look once you close this deal, you've got to know this. When you're doing your DD, once you close this deal, are you willing to be stuck with it for the next 10 years? Every month, every week, in that location, in that part of the city, okay? That construction type, that tenant type, are you willing to be stuck with this in the foreseeable future? Do not confuse this with do you want to live there? I buy apartments in the beginning for the first 20 years. I bought apartments that I would never have my family or myself live in. Today I'm buying apartments where I would go move in the property myself. But in the beginning, you probably won't be looking at that kind of property and I advise that you don't. Once you've established that this is the type of real estate and the type of deal that you want to be involved in, it's time to do your due diligence. Some are most of which could have already been done before you send your partner out or the person with the managing inspection kind of hat out to the property. Due diligence means reasonable steps taken by a person to satisfy a legal requirement, especially in buying or selling something. In this case, it's not a legal requirement. The bank's going to want to see your due diligence. The bank's going to require that you do a proper due diligence. The bank, in fact, might do their own due diligence. Why? Because they want to know you're buying a deal, 65% leverage, they're going to lend you the 65%. They want to know does the property need a roof? Does the property need floors? Does the property need a new clubhouse? And by the way, if not now, when will the property need these things? When doing your due diligence, some of the requirement is for you and your certainty. Some of it will be for the lender and their certainty. Others will be for the investors if you're bringing investors in. You have to know it's your responsibility in buying or selling anything that both parties would do their proper due diligence. When I sell a property, I do due diligence on the buyer that's buying the product from me. Can they close? Do they have the money? Do they have the funding? Have they bought other deals? How were they in those deals? Did they retrade the deals? Do your damn due diligence. So we want to consider due diligence as reasonable steps taken by person or persons in order to satisfy whether you as a real estate investor want to move forward or not. What to look for in your due diligence? In launching your due diligence, you'll want to look at everything, including anything that will contribute to the potential downside and always balance the potential upside of the property. You have to understand the worst case scenario so you can determine if you're prepared emotionally, physically, and financially to handle this property. You also want to understand how you will exit the deal, what it will need when you go to sell. If I know I'm buying a property that three or four years from now, I'm going to position myself to sell in three or four years and it needs a roof when I go to sell. I want to do the roof now and have myself, my tenants, and my investors benefit from a new roof now rather than waiting 48 months and doing it right before I sell. So you want to understand how you'll exit and if you can get a loan for that deal now and will the new buyer be able to get a loan for it in the future. But both are important to assessing. The due diligence assesses whether or not you can close and if the next guy can close and if you can even get a loan on it. Not to mention whether or not you'll be profitable in the short term and the long run. Look due diligence when done right gives you the bank and the investors the confidence to move forward with a deal. It's that important. That's why we do our due diligence faster than most all companies. I don't want to drag out my due diligence. Listen to what I'm saying. You have to get on that property and as quickly as possible. If you have a 30 day due diligence period, your goal should be to do that in 18 to 21 days and let the seller know I'm ready, I'm ready to go or I have to walk and I didn't want to waste or use all my due diligence. In my LOIs, I'll outline the basics of my due diligence and I get started immediately. Meaning 20 to 21 days or 30 days or 35 days or 21 business days or 21 total days, whatever the case is and I stick to that number. Anything longer, by the way, you might want to consider any kind of vacations, July 4th, all the vacations we have in this country, Thanksgiving day might interrupt your ability to have your inspections done. Anything longer and it may seem like you're not confident in your ability to close this deal. In this process, you'll want to ask several things. Don't ask without looking. Ask for something and look for validation. First income statements. Look for both the real ones and the wrong ones. Look for the ones that are legit and look for the boogie booze. Okay? Whatever the broker gave you is a boogie boo. It was created by a broker. It's called a pro forma. It suggests that the property will do something in the future. If your real estate broker could tell the future, they would be in the stock market, not in the real estate market. By the way, if they were that good, it's trust in their pro forma, they would have bought the deal themselves. Look at the income statement and look for the real validity, the real verification of income statements. The wrong ones tend to be the broker, no offense brokers. You got a job to do. You can't predict the future. You had to put something on your pro forma and also watch out for the mom and pop income statements that end in zeros. If you followed any of my other programs, my finance programs particularly, you've heard me talk about don't trust zeros. Financial statements typically, expenses, utilities, reimbursements, loss to lease, concessions, do not end in zeros. Any operator that rounds off the income and the expenses to be $21,000 even, $16,000, $12,400 is lazy at best and a liar at worst. If you keep to that simple concept, you know you're looking at an income statement that was made up. It doesn't mean it's bad by the way. It just means you're dealing with someone that doesn't always tell the complete truth. They're sloppy. They exaggerate. And by the way, the exaggeration might work against them. No one's expenses will be $21,000.00. No one makes $5,000 exactly on laundry and if it happens, it happens once in your career. Second thing you'll want to look at is previous inspections. If you can grab everything from the last sale, particularly in the last three years, that will be great. Anything done since they've been doing work, ask the seller to provide you with any previous inspections, title searches, appraisals, anything like that. Three, environmental checks. Anything regarding some environmental history on the property, oil, gas or any kind of environmental problems. Management roles. You need to see every tenant when they moved out on paper and when their lease expires. Review to ensure every lease is legitimate, trust me, sellers will and can and have mocked up leases that don't exist. Make sure there's no surprises like random concessions or agreements or lack of background checks because this is your revenue line. Your income depends on doing a proper due diligence on your leases. The existing debt on the building, including their current loans or loans, are any outstanding commitments to the property. Also look for rehab history. Any overview of any money put into the property. Lots of sellers will brag, we spent $1 million, we spent $7,000 a unit. Come on, man, give it up. Rehab history, an overview of all the money put into the property and documentation. All of the leases must have proper audits. They're usually turned over during the walkthroughs of the property. This is where you need a financial person, a very detailed person to go through every single lease and match it up with the tenant. Do a spreadsheet. When are most of the expirations happening? If you see 50% of the property, leases expiring in three months, that is a problem for you. You're going to close in three months from now, you're going to have half your property move out. That means your seller loaded the property with tenants preparing to sell at the appropriate time to blow up or balloon the income statement knowing in three months after they close, you'd have a problem. As you raise rents, as you rent more properties, as you fix things, guess what's going up? The expenses are going up. Spenses tend to go up as income goes up. This is a crucial piece of your due diligence process. Beyond that, they're the environmental issues. Look ultimately, your goal is to paint a full picture of your property, to know exactly what you're getting, to know why you can move forward, to know what is going to cost you to move forward. This is one of the due diligence items investors gloss over. Why? They're afraid or they're overwhelmed. They worry about debt. They think it'll derail the deal so they just ignore it. At first I didn't think much of it. I attributed it to the natural churn of the real estate. However, I quickly realized that the seller pulled a little quickie on me. I should have known. In days they went from 90% to 96% occupancy. Doesn't seem like a bunch. Show me the trailing 12. Give me a layout of your trailing 12 income statement. If it's a mom and pop seller this thing is going to look like one page. It's going to be made up. It won't be a spreadsheet. It won't be professional. That's good. If I could go back further than 12 months, a year ago, two years ago, three years ago, I'm going to check everything out lease wise. Because there's two kinds of due diligence. There's one that the bank needs and one that I need. I need to know. I need something for the bank so they can give me a loan and feel good about the debt. I need another one so I can feel good about the loan and feel good about the debt and feel good about the deal. The one I need, by the way, is more important than the one the bank needs.
